subject: Religious and Moral Education
sub_strand: THE PURPOSE AND USEFULNESS OF GOD'S CREATION
content_standard: B9/JHS3 1.1.1: Describe and explain the purpose and usefulness of God's creation
indicator_description: Identify the purpose and usefulness of God's creation
examplars:
1. Guide learners to recall the creation stories they learned at B8/JHS2. With Think-Pair Share, learners recollect lessons learned from the creation stories. [cite: 4183]
2. Identify the purpose and usefulness of God's creation. E.g. Humankind - to serve/worship God, care for other creatures (stewardship), etc.; Animals - security, companionship, food, clothing; Plants food, medicine, shelter, beautification; Water bodies food, transportation, irrigation, electricity, domestic and industrial use; Heavenly bodies - light, energy, time and seasons. [cite: 4183]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
sub_strand: THE PURPOSE AND USEFULNESS OF GOD'S CREATION
content_standard: B9/JHS3 1.1.1: Describe and explain the purpose and usefulness of God's creation
indicator_description: Explain how the complex nature of things created by God reveals His nature
examplars:
1. Learners brainstorm on how creation reveals God's complex nature. E.g. There is design in nature as can be seen from changes in seasons and what is done in each season, and the beauty in nature e.g. flowers, birds, rivers etc.). These show God as a planner and designer; All things created depend on each other for survival. This shows the caring nature of God. [cite: 4189]
2. In groups, learners discuss what the world would be like without key elements of God's creation and present it in class. [cite: 4189]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 1.2.1: Explain why it is important to care for the environment and how to do so
indicator_description: Describe human activities that destroy the environment
examplars:
1. Take learners on a nature walk. Learners to observe and note down human activities that destroy the environment. [cite: 4192]
2. Learners look at pictures and video on the negative effects of human activities on the environment. [cite: 4192]
3. Based on findings from the nature walk and the video/pictures learners identify human activities that destroy the environment. For example: Illegal tree felling, Illegal sand winning, Illegal mining, Bush burning, Over grazing, Over hunting, Hunting when hunting is banned, Water pollution, Weeding around water bodies, Improper fishing methods. [cite: 4192]
4. Discuss how the above human activities destroy the environment. [cite: 4197]
5. Debate on the motion "The benefits of tree felling outweigh the negative effects." [cite: 4197]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 1.2.1: Explain why it is important to care for the environment and how to do so
indicator_description: Identify and describe how indigenous Ghanaian culture helps in taking care of the environment
examplars:
1. Identify aspects of the environment in learners' locality regarded as sacred, and the rationale behind it. E.g. Earth goddess (Asase Yaa/Asase Afua), Water bodies (Bosompo, Nai, Bosomtwe), Totemic animals, Sacred forests (Nananompo). [cite: 4197, 4200]
2. Discuss the importance of the environment based on these beliefs. Trees around water bodies are not felled; Hunting not allowed in sacred forests; Fishing bans during certain seasons; Farming not allowed around water bodies; Certain animals not killed. [cite: 4200, 4203]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 1.2.1: Explain why it is important to care for the environment and how to do so
indicator_description: Discuss reasons for taking care for the environment.
examplars:
1. Demonstrate ways of protecting the environment: Reforestation, Proper farming practices, Proper disposal of waste, Proper mining practices. [cite: 4203]
2. Explain why indigenous Ghanaians take care of the environment: God reveals Himself through environment; Humans related to environment; Gods reside in nature; Environment linked to community history; Humans depend on environment for survival. [cite: 4203]
3. Dramatise how to apply lessons from indigenous Ghanaian attitudes towards the environment. [cite: 4203]
4. Project Work: Divide learners into groups to take care for the school's environment, e.g. planting trees, growing grass and desilting gutters. [cite: 4203]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 2.1.1: Understand the relevance of, and the need to participate in, religious festivals
indicator_description: State the meaning and types of festivals
examplars:
1. Brainstorm and explain the meaning of festivals. [cite: 4206]
2. Identify and explain the types of festivals in Ghana: Agricultural Festivals (Bakatue, Yam Festival) and Ancestral/Historical Festivals (Bugumchugu, Hogbetsotsoza, Christmas, Eid-Adha). [cite: 4206]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 2.1.1: Understand the relevance of, and the need to participate in, religious festivals
indicator_description: Describe the activities in festivals celebrated in the three main religions in Ghana
examplars:
1. Discuss the acts of preparation towards the celebration of festivals in Ghana. [cite: 4213]
2. List and explain the preparatory acts: ban on noise making, public appearance of religious leaders, cleaning up exercises and abstinence from sex. [cite: 4213]
3. Describe the main activities in the festivals: Family re-union, Exchange of gifts and greetings, Public worship, etc. [cite: 4213]
4. Dramatise the main activities that take place in one festival. [cite: 4213]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 2.1.1: Understand the relevance of, and the need to participate in, religious festivals
indicator_description: Identify and explain the social, religious and moral relevance of festivals in the three main religions and relate them to daily life
examplars:
1. Identify and explain the importance of festivals: Social (brings people together, planning development), Religious (reminds of relationship with God/ancestors, thanksgiving), Moral (remember exemplary lives of heroes, sharing with needy). [cite: 4216]
2. Learners dramatise how they apply religious and moral lessons from festivals in their daily life. [cite: 4216]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 3.1.1: Identify and apply ways people with different religions can co-exist peacefully
indicator_description: Identify and explain examples of tolerant and intolerant communities
examplars:
1. Show pictures or video clips to learners on a) a community which has experienced religious tolerance and b) a community, which has experienced religious intolerance. [cite: 4219]
2. From the pictures or video clips learners discuss characteristics of the two communities. [cite: 4219]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 3.1.1: Identify and apply ways people with different religions can co-exist peacefully
indicator_description: Explain reasons why religious intolerance occurs and their effects on society and the individual
examplars: 1. In groups, learners identify the causes of religious intolerance (not learning about each other's religion, undermining beliefs, state bias, etc.) and its effects (intra/inter-religious wars, destruction of property, disunity). [cite: 4226]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 3.1.1: Identify and apply ways people with different religions can co-exist peacefully
indicator_description: Identify and explain ways in which people with diverse religious backgrounds can live peacefully
examplars:
1. In groups, learners talk about ways people with diverse religious background can live peacefully (learning about each other, respect, participation in ceremonies). [cite: 4232]
2. In groups, learners discuss the need for people of diverse religious backgrounds to live in harmony (prevents tension, ensures development, respects rights). [cite: 4232]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
sub_strand: WOMEN IN RELIGION AND LEADERSHIP POSITIONS
content_standard: B9/JHS3 4.1.1: Recognise leadership role of women in society
indicator_description: Discuss the contributions of key women in the three major religions
examplars:
1. Identify key women: Christianity (Deborah, Mary), Islam (Khadijah, Aisha), Indigenous (Okomfo Oparebea, Yaa Asantewaa). [cite: 4237]
2. Describe the roles played by these women. [cite: 4237]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
sub_strand: WOMEN IN RELIGION AND LEADERSHIP POSITIONS
content_standard: B9/JHS3 4.1.1: Recognise leadership role of women in society
indicator_description: Discuss how to apply the moral lessons from the lives of these women in daily life
examplars:
1. Identify the role played by these women: medium used by God, helping spread religion, provided practical assistance, helped protect people. [cite: 4242]
2. In three groups, learners make a skit to demonstrate the life of a woman from each of the three religions. [cite: 4242]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
sub_strand: WOMEN IN RELIGION AND LEADERSHIP POSITIONS
content_standard: B9/JHS3 4.1.1: Recognise leadership role of women in society
indicator_description: Identify and explain the contributions of women to the development of Ghana
examplars:
1. Learners brainstorm to identify key women and their contributions in national development: Politics (MPs, Ministers), Legal (Chief Justice), Education (Directors), Health (Doctors), Sports, Agriculture. [cite: 4245]
2. Create a poster about a woman who has/has had a prominent role in the development of Ghanaian society and share with the class. [cite: 4245]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 5.1.1: Demonstrate an understanding that good deeds attract reward but bad deeds attract punishment
indicator_description: Describe the basis for good deeds and reward
examplars:
1. Guide learners to recollect the moral teachings learned at Indicator 8.5.2.1.1. [cite: 4248]
2. Explain the concepts "good deeds" and "reward". [cite: 4248]
3. Identify acts considered as good deeds: Assisting elderly/weak, relinquishing seat to pregnant/sick, assisting children crossing road. [cite: 4248]
4. Explain the need for performance of good deeds. [cite: 4248]
5. Identify and explain types of rewards: verbal (thank you), social (good name), physical (certificates), emotional (embrace). [cite: 4253, 4254, 4255]
6. Discuss the importance of rewarding people: encouragement, ensures hard work, emulate good deeds. [cite: 4256]
7. In a school event, learners should act a play on the theme ‘good deeds have rewards.’ [cite: 4256]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 5.1.1: Demonstrate an understanding that good deeds attract reward but bad deeds attract punishment
indicator_description: Identify and explain acts that attract punishment
examplars:
1. Explain the meanings of “bad deed” and punishment. [cite: 4257]
2. Identify acts that are considered as bad deeds: telling lies, false witness, stealing, disobedience. [cite: 4259]
3. Mention the various types of punishment: Reprimanding, Physical, Withdrawal of support, Imprisonment, Fines. [cite: 4260]
4. State and explain the reasons for punishment: reformation, retribution, deterrence, protection, law and order. [cite: 4260]
5. Project Work: Do a research in your community on the life of one person the community punished for a bad deed and explain three moral lessons. [cite: 4260, 4261]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 5.1.1: Demonstrate an understanding that good deeds attract reward but bad deeds attract punishment
indicator_description: Outline the stages of repentance
examplars:
1. Explain the term repentance. [cite: 4264]
2. Identify stages of repentance: Realisation and acceptance, regret, request for pardon, promise not to repeat. [cite: 4264, 4266]
3. Discuss the importance of repentance: good relationship, draws to God, relief of conscience. [cite: 4266]
4. Use the story of the prodigal son in the Bible to dramatise the need for repentance, forgiveness and reconciliation. [cite: 4266]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 6.1.1: Develop skills in managing time profitably
indicator_description: Explain the meaning of the terms “time”, “leisure” and “idleness”.
examplars: 1. Through Think-Pair-Share explain the meaning of “time”, “leisure” and “idleness”. [cite: 4270, 4271, 4274]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 6.1.1: Develop skills in managing time profitably
indicator_description: Demonstrate how to plan and use time wisely:
examplars:
1. Learners identify and discuss the teachings of the three religions on time. [cite: 4278]
2. Learners to prepare personal comprehensive time table on their daily activities for one month. [cite: 4281]

subject: Religious and Moral Education
content_standard: B9/JHS3 6.1.1: Develop skills in managing time profitably
indicator_description: Explain the need for leisure
examplars:
1. Discuss how to use your leisure profitably: sports, reading, gardening, tourism, music. [cite: 4290, 4291]
2. Explain the need to have leisure: Relaxation, Divine law, Reflection, Meditation, Helping others. [cite: 4291, 4292]
3. Prepare personal programmes for hobbies and others out of school activities. [cite: 4292]
